text: Before my 3-year-old was born, I bought theBright Starts See & Play Auto Mirrorbecause I thought it was cute and didn't think that I would have this much trouble with baby mirrors for the car. For whatever reason, it just wouldn't hold steady on the center headrest in our 2011 Honda Odyssey. I even tried using other objects like foam pipe insulation to try and steady it, but nothing worked. I ended up donating it after my sister gave me her Fisher Price musical auto mirror, which is no longer available on Amazon. I can't remember if it wouldn't fit on the headrest or if it couldn't be situated properly, but I donated that, too, and just decided to live without a mirror.When I had my second baby last year, I decided I wanted a mirror. Even though I was a second-time mom, I still felt a little paranoid not being able to see him, and I found myself reaching back while driving to feel for his hand to check that he was ok when he was quiet. I also disliked not being able to see if he'd fallen asleep. I did a little research and was deciding between this mirror and a Britax one. I chose Brica over Britax due to price, reviews, and availability in a local store so that I could see it in person before I bought it. I never got a chance to install it in the Odyssey, but I recently installed it in our 2014 Volvo XC60. We are using it with a rear-facingBritax Boulevard 70-G3 Convertible Car Seat Seat, Onyxright behind the front passenger seat (so not in the middle). To install it, we put the baby in his car seat, and I sat in the driver's seat while my husband installed it.Pros:-Installation took all of about 15 seconds, and once we found a position that I liked, all my husband had to do was climb in the back of the car to tighten the mirror on the headrest. The only reason he had to do that was because our older son's car seat prevented him from accessing the middle of the back seat.-The mirror is so wide that I can easily see my baby's reflection really well both in my rear view mirror and directly from the driver's seat. My husband sat in the passenger seat and said that if he turned around and leaned over towards the driver's seat, he could see the baby's reflection, too.-The mirror is tall enough that it won't need any readjusting as my baby grows taller.-The mount is sturdy enough that the mirror doesn't shift when we hit bumps in the road.-The mount has multiple options for different types of cars, including those without center headrests.-I no longer reach back to check on my baby while driving, which is safer for everyone.Cons:-The mirror portion itself is a little cloudy (and remained so even after using a little Windex on it), but I guess that's what you get for not using actual glass due to safety. Honestly it's not even noticeable when you're sitting in the front seat looking at it. I can see my baby just fine.All in all, I'm very happy that I decided to research this out and came upon this mirror! I honestly have no real complaints!

text: We had a plastic mat underneath our high chair for the first two years for our daughter... It does not stay flat -  Every time we pull the chair in and out it bunches up and wrinkles.  Though we can straighten it out, it requires constant attention and re-laying. It's pretty and probably would be good for that, but fails as a under table mat to keep things clean.

text: This was superb to start with...until it developed a fault. Picture was great, for the size of screen. Sound was stellar. We purchased this to keep an eye on our toddler and baby, who were sleeping in the same room. Since we were co sleeping with the baby (crib attached to our bed), and our toddler was sleeping in our bed, we were concerned that he might either disturb her while she was sleeping, or else try to play with her, put something (covers, pillow, etc) on her trying to "help" cover her, etc. We didn't want the safety risk involved with a toddler and baby with full access unsupervised. However, we held back from purchasing one of these initially, due to the cost. However, after 2 or 3 months of having to hop up and down literally every five minutes, until we knew they were both asleep, crossing the house back and forth each time, we decided it would be worth investing the money to just be able to glance up at the screen. This was really very good but after a month or two developed a fault, whereby the screen started going fuzzy - slowly - until it was just snow...it stopped working on the battery altogether, and then would only work when plugged in for about two hours before slowly phasing out into snow. I have a feeling that this may have just been a faulty item, individually, but we had to return it. We ended up changing the sleeping arrangements, slightly, and put the toddler in with the other kids, and he did just fine, so we didn't end up needing to purchase another one. I would highly recommend this or other products like it, if you want the peace of mind of being able to see and hear your children when not in the same room with them. The quality of this item was really good, and aside from developing the fault, would probably have lasted us many years. The touchscreen was ok. It could be better. For anyone used to the smooth perfection of high quality touchphones, this will be a definite step down, but for someone new to them, it will seem pretty amazing. It was not all that responsive, and you have to press pretty hard down and scroll to get it to do much, but we didn't purchase it for this feature, so it was fine.

text: After reading many reviews, I ordered this bib to try to get to a point where we don't have to change our 8-month-old baby's clothes after every meal. It's not a bad bib, but it does not achieve that goal. On the good side of things, the bib tightly closes around the neck to protect against drips down the neck, it is easy to clean, and the pocket does stay open to catch mess as advertised.Unfortunately, in using it over the last couple weeks, we have discovered a few downsides. Our baby is tall for his age, and there is a gap between the bottom of the bib and his tray, and it seems like food and stains always get in this gap and mess up his clothes. Worst of all, our son likes to chew on the silicone material of the bib, and often does so. This means that anything in the pocket dumps all over his clothes, resulting in a terrible mess! So far we have still had to change his clothes after every meal using this product. I will keep looking for something else.

text: Yes, scratchy!  I was quite surprised by the rough texture of the top layer of the sheet.  It feels like very cheap, low thread count material, not nearly as soft as most bedsheets.  When my daugher used this sheet, she had a skin rash and started getting a bold spot on the back of her head.  It's possible, of course, that these were unrelated to the sheet.  However, both problems improved substantially when we removed it and put her on a softer, normal bed sheet.Also, I agree with another poster that this could be a dangerous product, especially if your baby is older and moves around her crib a lot.  There is no way to tuck the sides under, as with a regular sheet, so a baby could find her way under the sheet and suffocate.  Keep in mind, too, that undoing the snaps is very easy--so easy, a baby could do it!  The elastic bands connecting the sheet to the crib also make me nervous.  Could an older baby pinch or strangle herself in these?My daughter is now six months, and rolls around her crib.  I won't be regularly using this sheet anymore.  But, there is an upside.  This product is AMAZINGLY easy to use.  Snap, snap--that's it.  There is no lifting the mattress.  And it is waterproof underneath (unlike a certain zipper sheet, I could name), so in an emergency you don't have to worry about the mattress.  Recently, my daughter had a stomach virus, diarrhea everywhere.  To make matters worse, I caught it and was too light-headed to lift a 20lb crib mattress.  This sheet was perfect.

text: The Graco Pack 'N Play Playard is "relative easy" to put together. It is not easy enough that my Mother could figure it out, but I was able to. Although it is not too difficult to figure out how to put the playard together, it takes some time to do it. So this is not something I would be able to break down and take to Grandma's house everyday.While I love the idea of the reversible napper and changer, I find that it does not work well. First, the height at which the change is so low, that I have to lean over a lot to change my dear son's diaper. I am about 5' 6" and I found my back hurting after just a couple diaper changes. Second, when you flip over the changer/napper, the napper sits at a higher position than the changer. This makes no sense to me as you would want the changer higher to protect your back during diaper changes, and the napper to be lower just in case your baby wakes up learns to turn (and accidentally off the napper) for the first time when your not around.One feature I do like is that the bottom platform can be raised. This allows the baby, before he can stand, to lay/nap in the playard. Then when the baby is old enough to stand, you lower the platform to the bottom of the playard as a playpen.The three positions where you can put your baby, napper, raised platform, lower platform, allow this playard to be used from birth to toddler. However I just found that napper/changer didn't really work for me. Currently my son naps using the raised platform and I can see him as a toddler in the lower platform.PROS:You can raise/lower platform to work with the age of the babyIt can be stored in a compact carrying caseCONS:The changer is too low to prevent back aches while changing a diaperIt takes some time to break down/put together the playard

text: I purchased two of these locks nearly two years ago, one for our pantry door and one for the laundry room door.  Our pantry door opens outward so we didn't have any issue with that lock and it has worked fine.  The laundry room door opens inward and therefore the door frame got in the way of installing the lock.  My husband was deployed at the time and I was going insane trying to keep my then 1-year old from pushing every button on the washer and dryer all day long (throwing fits whenever I pulled him away), so I chiseled away about 1/4" of the door frame in order to install the lock.  It has since worn the wood down to a smooth finish, popped off, and left a thin layer of adhesive tape on the door that I get to deal with now.  So, it worked, but not without some difficulty on my part and damage to the door.Although I haven't yet tried to remove the adhesive and I can't tell you how difficult it is to remove (I'm guessing it will take some good scraping and lots of Goo Gone), I would recommend this lock only for door knobs that don't have an adjacent door frame to deal with.  It seems these were poorly designed as they are too bulky to fit.

text: We've been using a Summer Infant Day & Night Handheld Color Video Monitor 1.8" Screen and were quite happy with its performance when set up close to baby's bed, but when we mounted the camera in a corner to get a view of the whole bedroom (baby is now toddler), it just wasn't sufficient.When we finally decided to upgrade with Baby #2 on the way (thought it was a good time to get a system that could support multiple cameras and had a larger viewing screen), we initially decided on the Samsung Wireless Video Security Monitoring System w/ 3.5" LCD after doing some research. Picture quality during daytime viewing was fine (not great), but the night vision was dismal (the screen was mostly black and we couldn't really see anything at all). We did not test the camera at close range -- this was only mounted in the corner, near the ceiling, about 10 ft. away from toddler in his bed. Also, even with the camera sensitivity set to the highest possible, we could not get the monitor to engage when "asleep" when there was noise in the toddler's room (as it was supposed to do). The stand on the monitor was poorly designed, and we found the controls to be somewhat cumbersome.Because night vision was one of our #1 priorities and the Samsung failed there, we returned the Samsung in favor of the Summer Infant BabyTouch, though it was considerably more expensive. We now love the Summer. Not only does it support up to 4 different cameras (like the Samsung), but the cameras MOVE (have the ability to pan around the room). Night vision is amazing, even mounted in the same spot as we had the Samsung (about 10 feet away from our target). Daytime picture is also pretty good. If you're expecting to get TV quality out of a baby monitor, you're not going to find it, and this unit does not have it (neither does the Samsung). But you can certainly see what you need to see. The controls are easy and intuitive, and the touch screen is great. We love that you can turn the video portion off to conserve battery, but still have continuous audio (unlike the Samsung). Video turns on and off with a single button touch. The camera silently pans around the room as well using controls on the touch screen, which the Samsung does not do.If night vision at varying distances is important to you, then the Summer BabyTouch is going to suit your needs better than this Samsung unit. All in all, the Summer is also just easier to use and was worth the extra cost over the Samsung.

text: The Diaper Genie II and it's brother the Diaper Genie II Elite are barely related.  We bought a Diaper Genie II to supplement our Diaper GEnie II Elite. What we didn't realize was that the foot pedal was how the ingenious airlock system works on the Elite. On the regular Diaper Genie II, you have to push the diaper through the hole.  first, it takes quite a bit of pressure.  Secondly, you have to push it WITH the diaper - and you can imagine that you might get your hands dirty doing that.  So, we were disappointed as soon as we set it up and saw how it works - but it was too late to return the product. So now we're stuck with it.Updated ...We have been using it for a month and have gotten used to it. I'd still recommend the Elite II, but this product isn't bad for a 2nd diaper disposal unit.  You do get used to the amount of pressure required to push down a diaper and as new parents, we've learned how to roll up the diaper into a tiny, compact package.  It's been about a month and I haven't gotten my hands dirty yet.

text: We had a big family vacation with seven of us on the same flight, including our four year old daughter. Travel in groups is always a frenzy, and the last thing we wanted to do was add schlepping a car seat to the mix.I had seen this harness previously and had been mentally tracking it, and as our trip approached I did more research and decided to buy it. Bottom line is that it works like a charm.We were lucky enough to have seats with no row behind us so we did not have to worry about disturbing a stranger for the initial setup, although the impact to the people behind you is only cosmetic (there's a strap going under their tray table when it's in the upright position).One of the flight attendants came by and said that in 20 years of flying she'd never seen one before. I told her that her airline (American) states on their site that they are approved for use on their planes, and they even sell the harnesses there. I was worried she might question the device, but she was merely curious and seemed fascinated by it.Our daughter had no issues with it, which may be in part because it fastens similarly to the straps of a car seat. I'll echo other comments that the lack of a between-the-legs strap seems less safe than having one, but in actual use she felt quite secure.Bottom line, I highly recommend this to parents traveling with kids. It folds up into a small pouch, it's simple to use, and it makes travel significantly easier.
